content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 6, 2017

Darcy C. Vaughn
Assistant City Attorney
City of Ukiah
405 West Perkins Street
Ukiah, CA 95482

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-180

Dear Mr. Vaughn:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Ukiah City Councilmember Maureen Mulheren regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in the governmental decision on whether the City of Ukiah will permit cannabis-related businesses within the City?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in that decision because the decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmember's financial interests based on the facts presented.

FACTS

You are an Assistant City Attorney for the City of Ukiah and the authorized representative of Ukiah City Councilmember Maureen Mulheren. The City has formed an ad hoc committee to consider the City's policy objectives regarding cannabis regulation. The Councilmember is a member of this committee, and the committee makes recommendations to the City Council about

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-180
Page No. 2

various City policies relating to cannabis. The committee and then the City Council in turn will
soon consider whether to authorize cannabis-related businesses within the City.

In her private capacity, the Councilmember is an independent insurance agent and the sole
proprietor of her insurance agency. The Councilmember's insurance agency sells a variety of types
of insurance policies, including liability and workers' compensation insurance, to local businesses.
The Councilmember has publicized the fact that her insurance agency sells commercial liability
policies classified for cannabis-related businesses by posting this information to her insurance
agency's website, attending cannabis business trade shows, and by relying on word of mouth.
Including the Councilmember's insurance agency, there are at least 15 insurance agencies within
the City. The Councilmember's insurance agency's website does not indicate that the
Councilmember has special expertise with regard to the City's cannabis policies, or that the agency
has any unique advantage with respect to providing insurance to cannabis-related businesses.

The State's recently enacted "Medicinal and Adult-Use Cannabis Regulation and Safety
Act" ("MAUCRSA") requires a cannabis-related business that is a "distributor" to be "bonded and
insured at a minimum level established by the licensing authority." (Stats. 2017, Ch. 27, §9.) The agencies charged with implementing the MAUCRSA (the Bureau of Cannabis Control, the
Department of Health, and the Department of Food and Agriculture) are currently undergoing the
rulemaking process to adopt regulations pursuant to it. The City Council's decision on whether to
permit cannabis-related businesses within the City does not impose any new insurance
requirements. If the City permits cannabis-related businesses within the City, only those cannabis-
related businesses within the City that are new commercial cannabis distributors would be newly
required to be bonded and carry insurance under the MAUCRSA.

The Councilmember has sold one commercial general liability policy to a medical cannabis
cultivation business to date, that business is located outside the City, and you confirmed that that
business has no intention of doing business within City limits on August 22, 2017. Although the
Councilmember has not sold a commercial liability insurance policy to any other cannabis-related
business to date, her insurance agency has received increased inquiries about cannabis-related
policies since the State's passage of Proposition 64, the "Control, Regulate and Tax Adult Use of
Marijuana Act" ("AUMA"), in November of 2016. Among other things, the AUMA authorized
adult use of cannabis, subject to certain conditions and limits, under State law.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a
"financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified
in that section. Section 87103 identifies the following financial interests:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

File No. A-17-180
Page No. 3

• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b).)

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the official within 12
months prior to the decision.² (Section 87103(c).)

• Any donor of, or intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or more
in value provided to, received by, or promised to the official within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(e).)

• An interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See Section
87103.)

With respect to the decision on whether to permit cannabis-related businesses within the
City, the Councilmember has an interest in her insurance agency, interests in sources of income to
her agency, and an interest in her personal finances and those of immediate family members.³

Foreseeability and Materiality

A decision's financial effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably
foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).)
And an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
interest. (Ibid.) If the interest is not a named party in or the subject of the decision, the decision's
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Based on the facts presented, none of the Councilmember's interests would be a named
party or the subject of the decision on whether to permit cannabis-related businesses within the
City.⁴ Therefore, the effect of that decision on each of the Councilmember's interests is reasonably
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical.

___________________________

² Section 82030 sets forth the Act's definition of "income" and provides that income "of an individual also
includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater."

³ Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a personal financial effect, and
subdivision (c) of that regulation provides that if a decision also affects an official's interest in a business entity,
materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business interests. Because the decision regarding
whether to permit cannabis-related businesses within the City may affect the Councilmember's business interest in her
insurance agency, we do not further analyze her interest in her personal finances.

⁴ If any source of income to the Councilmember is a named party or the subject of a decision, she should seek
additional advice.

File No. A-17-180
Page No. 4

Insurance Agency

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find that it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of that business. That regulation also provides various examples of types of decisions that may contribute to a change in the value of the business. (Ibid.) Of those examples, the only one relevant to the facts presented is the example of "a decision that would 'increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies.'" (Regulation 18702.1(b)(3).)

The Councilmember's insurance agency provides insurance products to its clients, and the Councilmember has publicized the fact that her agency sells commercial liability policies classified for cannabis-related businesses. Because the MAUCRSA requires cannabis distributors to carry insurance coverage, if the City permits cannabis-related businesses within City limits, only those cannabis-related businesses within the City that are new cannabis distributors would be newly required to carry insurance coverage. Nonetheless, the decision at issue is of the type of decision described in Regulation 18702.1(b)(3) because it would increase the need for insurance products that the Councilmember's insurance agency supplies.

However, this increased need for insurance products from new cannabis distributors within the City does not conclusively determine whether the decision at issue would have a material effect on the Councilmember's business interest in her insurance agency. Under Regulation 18702. 1(b), a decision's effect is material only if it "would contribute to a change in the value of the business."

The City Council's decision on whether to permit cannabis-related businesses within the City does not impose any new insurance requirements. As noted above, if the City permits cannabis-related businesses within the City, only those cannabis-related businesses within the City that are new cannabis distributors, a small subset of all cannabis-related businesses, would be required to carry insurance coverage under the MAUCRSA. Including the Councilmember's insurance agency within the City, there are at least 15 insurance agencies within the City, and those 15 insurance agencies are likely to compete for any potential increase in the need for insurance products from any new cannabis distributors within the City that would be required to carry insurance under MAUCRSA.

In the Peterson Advice Letter, No. A-17-111, we found that the Act prohibited a city councilmember, who in her private capacity worked as a real estate broker for her own real estate brokerage, from taking part in decisions relating to a land use ordinance that would determine the zone within the city in which commercial medical cannabis businesses would be permitted, noting that the decisions at issue would "increase or decrease the need for products and services" that the councilmember's brokerage provides.

In the Peterson Advice Letter, supra, the commercial cannabis zoning ordinance at issue provided the councilmember's real estate brokerage a competitive advantage because the brokerage was located within 500 feet of the proposed zone. Here, the decision on whether to permit cannabis-related businesses within the City would not provide Councilmember Mulheren's insurance agency with a competitive advantage for the potential increased need for insurance products versus the other 14 insurance agencies also located within the City.

File No. A-17-180
Page No. 5

In the Peterson Advice Letter, supra, the councilmember's real estate brokerage's website
advertised the councilmember as an expert on the city's cannabis ordinances, and indicated that the
real estate brokerage was located "right in the midst of all the activity." In contrast, Councilmember
Madlmen's insurance agency's website does not indicate that the Councilmember has special
expertise regarding the City's cannabis policies, or that the Councilmember's insurance agency has
any unique competitive advantage with respect to providing insurance to new cannabis distributors
within the City. Therefore, the facts presented are distinguishable from those in the Peterson
Advice Letter, supra.

In light of the small subset of cannabis-related businesses that are new cannabis distributors
that would be required to carry insurance under the MAUCRSA if the City permits cannabis-related
businesses within the City, and the 14 other insurance agencies within the City that are also likely to
compete for that additional need for insurance products, if any, we find that the potential increase in
the need for insurance products at issue is not sufficiently substantial to contribute to a change in
the value in the value of the Councilmember's insurance agency. Accordingly, the decision on
whether to permit cannabis-related businesses within the City would not have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmember's insurance agency.³

Source-of-Income Interests

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable effect on an official's interest in a source of income, and provides that if the source is a
business, whether the decision's effect is material is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1.

The facts presented only identify one source of income to the Councilmember that may be
affected by the decision to permit cannabis-related businesses within the City; the Councilmember's
insurance agency's sole client that is a medical cannabis cultivation business.⁶ Because that medical
cannabis cultivation client is a business, and because that business is not explicitly involved in the
decision on whether to permit cannabis-related businesses within the City, whether the effect of that
decision on that business is material is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1(b). Applying that
provision again, the financial effect of the decision at issue on that medical cannabis cultivation
business is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find that it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of that business.

The medical cannabis cultivation business at issue is located outside the City, and has no
intention of doing business within the City. If the City permits cannabis-related businesses within
the City, and if new commercial cannabis cultivators are located within the City pursuant to that
authorization, those cultivators, as well as the Councilmember's insurance agency's medical
cannabis cultivation business client, would compete in regional or State-wide markets for cannabis
sales. In support of the preeminence of regional and State-wide markets for cannabis, we note that a
substantial portion of the cannabis produced in Mendocino County is not consumed there. Just as

---
³ However, if a decision on whether the City will impose additional insurance requirements on cannabis-
related businesses comes before the committee or the City Council, you should seek additional advice on behalf of the
Councilmember.

⁶ If the decision at issue would potentially have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any other
source of income to the Councilmember, you should seek additional advice on behalf of the Councilmember.

File No. A-17-180
Page No. 6

some wineries located nearby one another in the Napa Valley compete for regional, State-wide, nationwide, or international market share rather than local market share, commercial cannabis cultivators located nearby one another will also compete for regional or State-wide market share rather than local market share. Because there is sufficient regional and State-wide demand for cannabis, it is unnecessary for commercial cannabis cultivators located nearby one another to compete for local market share.

Therefore, a prudent person with sufficient information would not find that it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision on whether to permit cannabis-related businesses within the City would contribute to a change in the value of the Councilmember's insurance agency's medical cannabis cultivation business client. Accordingly, that decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on that medical cannabis cultivation business.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

Does the Act prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in the governmental decision on 
whether the City of Ukiah will permit cannabis-related businesses within the City?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in that decision because 
the decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
Councilmember's financial interests based on the facts presented.

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the 
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influei:ice a governmental decision in 
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a 
"financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified 
in that section. Section 87103 identifies the following financial interests: 
• 
Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103( d) ).  
• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the official within 12 
months prior to the decision.2 (Section 87103(c).) 
• Any donor of, or intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or more 
in value provided to, received by, or promised to the official within 12 months prior to the 
decision. (Section 87103(e).) 
• An interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See Section 
87103.) 
With respect to the decision on whether to permit cannabis-related businesses within the 
City, the Councilmember has an interest in her insurance agency, interests in sources of income to 
her agency, and an interest in her personal finances and those of immediate family members. 3 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A decision's financial effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) 
And an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, 
approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
interest. (Ibid.) If the interest is not a named party in or the subject of the decision, the decision's 
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
Based on the facts presented, none of the Councilmember's interests would be a named 
party or the subject of the decision on whether to permit cannabis-related businesses within the 
City.4 Therefore, the effect of that d_ecision on each of the Councilmember's interests is reas_onably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical.

includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, 
indirectly or beneficially, a I0•percent interest or greater." 
J Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a personal financial effect, and

materiality is detennined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business interests. Because the decision regarding 
whether to pennit cannabis-related businesses within the City may affect the Councilmember's business interest in her 
insurance agency, we do not further analyze her interest in her personal finances. 
4 If any source of income to the Councilmember is a named party or the subject of a decision, she should seek 
additional advice. 


Insurance Agency  
Regulation 18702.l(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest is

also provides various examples of types of decisions that may contribute to a change in the value of 
the business. (Ibid.) Of those examples, the only one relevant to the facts presented is the example

entity supplies." (Regulation 18702. l (b )(3 ). )

described in Regulation 18702.l(b)(3) because it would increase the need for insurance products 
that the Councilmember's insurance agency supplies.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in the governmental decision on 
whether the City of Ukiah will permit cannabis-related businesses within the City?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in that decision because 
the decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
Councilmember's financial interests based on the facts presented.
